name: Ads Click Aggregator
overview:
An ads click aggregator collects billions of raw click events from ad networks, deduplicates them, aggregates
counts per ad per time window, and exposes query APIs for billing and analytics dashboards. The system must
handle extreme write throughput (hundreds of thousands of events per second), guarantee exactly-once
counting to prevent billing fraud, and serve low-latency read queries over aggregated data. Late-arriving events
(due to mobile offline scenarios) must be handled gracefully. The pipeline must be fault-tolerant, horizontally
scalable, and auditable.

inScope: Ingest raw click events (ad_id, user_id, timestamp, metadata)
Deduplicate clicks within a configurable window (e.g., 1 hour)
Aggregate click counts per ad per minute/hour/day
Query aggregated counts by ad_id and time range
Support late-arriving events up to 24 hours

items: Handle 500k clicks/sec ingestion with durable, fault-tolerant buffering
Exactly-once counting for billing-grade accuracy — undercounting or overcounting has financial impact
Aggregation query latency <100ms for dashboard range queries
Support late-arriving events up to 24 hours after event time
Horizontally scalable: add partitions/workers without downtime
Immutable raw event archive for audit and reprocessing

entities.name: Click Event
entities.schema:
{
  event_id: UUID,       // idempotency key
  ad_id: string,
  user_id: string,

  session_id: string,
  timestamp: ISO8601,   // client-side event time
  ip: string,
  user_agent: string,
  metadata: map<string,string>
}

entities.name: Aggregated Count
entities.schema:
{
  ad_id: string,
  window_start: timestamp,
  window_type: enum(MINUTE|HOUR|DAY),
  click_count: int64,
  unique_users: HyperLogLog,
  last_updated: timestamp
}

endpoints.request: Body: { event_id, ad_id, user_id, timestamp, metadata }

approaches.title: Synchronous DB Writes
approaches.description: Write each click directly to a relational database on the hot path. Simple to implement but the DB becomes a bottleneck at scale. A single MySQL instance handles ~5k writes/sec; at 500k clicks/sec this fails immediately. No buffering means any downstream slowness causes client timeouts.
approaches.tradeoff: DB cannot sustain write throughput; single point of failure.

approaches.title: Kafka + Stream Processor
approaches.description: Clients write to a Kafka topic partitioned by ad_id. A Flink/Spark Streaming job reads, deduplicates using a Redis Bloom filter, and writes aggregates to a time-series store. Kafka provides durable buffering and replay. Partitioning by ad_id ensures ordered processing per ad. Flink's event-time windows handle late arrivals via watermarks.
approaches.tradeoff: Operational complexity of Kafka + Flink cluster; requires expertise.

approaches.title: Kafka + Flink with Tiered Storage
approaches.description: Same as above but Kafka is configured with tiered storage (S3 offload) for long-term replay. Flink uses RocksDB state backend for large dedup windows. Aggregates are written to Apache Druid (OLAP) for fast range queries and to Cassandra for per-ad time-series lookups. A separate Lambda/batch job reconciles any missed late events daily.
approaches.tradeoff: Higher infra cost; Druid cluster management adds complexity.

approaches.title: Exact Dedup with Redis SET
approaches.description: Store event_id in a Redis SET with TTL equal to the dedup window. Works for small windows but Redis memory grows linearly with unique events. At 500k events/sec × 3600s = 1.8B keys per hour — infeasible.
approaches.tradeoff: Memory explosion at scale; Redis becomes bottleneck.

approaches.title: Bloom Filter per Time Bucket
approaches.description: Use a Bloom filter (Redis BF module) per 1-hour bucket. False positive rate ~0.1% is acceptable for click counting. Memory: ~1.2 GB per hour bucket for 1B events at 0.1% FPR. Rotate buckets hourly.
approaches.tradeoff: False positives cause slight undercounting; not suitable for billing-critical paths.

approaches.title: Flink Keyed State + Periodic Checkpoints
approaches.description: Flink maintains a keyed state (HashSet of event_ids) per ad_id partition. State is checkpointed to S3 every 30 seconds. Exactly-once semantics via Flink's two-phase commit sink. Dedup window is configurable per ad campaign. For billing, a secondary exact-dedup pass runs on raw S3 data nightly.
approaches.tradeoff: Flink state can grow large; requires careful state TTL configuration.

components.name: Click Ingest API
components.responsibility: Stateless HTTP service; validates events, publishes to Kafka with event_id as key

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Durable event log; partitioned by ad_id; tiered storage to S3 for replay

components.name: Flink Aggregator
components.responsibility: Consumes Kafka; deduplicates; computes tumbling/sliding windows; writes to Druid + Cassandra

components.name: Redis
components.responsibility: Bloom filter for fast dedup; hot aggregate cache for dashboard queries

components.name: Apache Druid
components.responsibility: OLAP store for ad-hoc range queries; pre-aggregated rollups

components.name: Cassandra
components.responsibility: Per-ad time-series store; partition key = ad_id; clustering key = window_start

components.name: S3 Raw Store
components.responsibility: Immutable raw event archive for audit and reprocessing

components.name: Reconciliation Job
components.responsibility: Nightly batch job re-aggregates from S3 to fix late arrivals and correct counts

questions.title: 2. Handling Late-Arriving Events
questions.content:
Mobile clients may buffer clicks offline and send them hours later. Flink's event-time processing with
watermarks handles this: the watermark advances based on the maximum observed event timestamp minus a
configurable allowed lateness (e.g., 24 hours). Events within the allowed lateness window update existing
aggregates; events beyond the window are routed to a side output for manual review.
// Flink watermark strategy
WatermarkStrategy
  .<ClickEvent>forBoundedOutOfOrderness(Duration.ofHours(24))
  .withTimestampAssigner((e, ts) -> e.getTimestamp())

questions.title: 3. Query Performance for Dashboards
questions.content:
Ad dashboards query click counts for a given ad over arbitrary time ranges. Apache Druid pre-aggregates data
at minute, hour, and day granularities using its segment architecture. Queries on Druid for a 30-day range over
a single ad_id complete in under 100ms because Druid scans only the relevant segments. For real-time data
(last 5 minutes), the dashboard queries Redis directly where the Flink job writes running totals.

questions.title: 4. Partitioning Strategy
questions.content:
Kafka topics are partitioned by ad_id using consistent hashing. This ensures all clicks for a given ad go to the
same partition, enabling stateful per-ad processing in Flink without cross-partition joins. Hot ads (viral
campaigns) can cause partition skew; we mitigate this by sub-partitioning hot ad_ids across multiple partitions
and merging in a downstream aggregation step.

questions.title: 5. Scalability and Capacity Planning
questions.content:
At 500k clicks/sec, Kafka needs ~50 partitions at 10k events/partition/sec. Each Flink task manager handles 5
partitions, requiring 10 task managers. Cassandra is sized for 1TB/day of aggregated data with a 90-day
retention, requiring a 90TB cluster with 3x replication = 270TB raw storage. Druid segments are compressed
~10:1, so 30 days of data fits in ~3TB.

keyFlows: ① Ad SDK → Ingest API → Kafka (partitioned by ad_id)
② Flink reads Kafka, deduplicates via Redis Bloom filter
③ Flink writes aggregates to Cassandra (time-series) + Druid (OLAP)
④ Raw events archived to S3; nightly Reconcile job corrects counts
⑤ Dashboard queries Query API → Cassandra (recent) or Druid (historical)

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Apache Flink's exactly-once semantics: how two-phase commit works with Kafka
sources and external sinks, and the role of checkpoints and savepoints in fault recovery.

Expert Note: Understand Apache Druid's architecture: how segments are created, indexed, and queried;
the role of the coordinator, broker, historical, and middleManager nodes.
Expert Note: Learn about HyperLogLog for approximate unique counting: why it uses only 12KB to
estimate cardinality with 2% error, and how to merge HLL sketches across time windows.
Expert Note: Study Kafka's tiered storage feature: how it offloads older segments to object storage (S3)
while keeping recent data on local SSDs, enabling cost-effective long-term retention.
Expert Note: Understand watermark strategies in stream processing: event time vs. processing time, how
watermarks propagate through a DAG, and the trade-off between latency and completeness.
